macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Mi Shemeit: Heirs, Female Minors, and the Estate Division Framework

micro_segments.title: Overview of Perek Mi Shemeit and the fundamental distinction between nechasim merubim and nechasim muatim
Explanation of the lien model: daughters as creditors on the estate with claims for support until bagrut
Definition of merubim: one penny more than the amount needed to support daughters until bagrut

macro_segments.title: Merubim Venismatu: When a Large Estate Shrinks and the Status of the Heirs' Portion

micro_segments.title: The pshita of merubim venismatu: the heirs retain their portion even when estate value drops
Two scenarios for shrinkage: market decline in stocks versus rising food prices, and their different implications
The conceptual debate: are daughters true creditors or is their portion protected via a debt-payment model?
Tosafot's protective principle: nachala de'oraisa motivates preserving some inheritance for the heirs even post-shrinkage

macro_segments.title: Muatim Venisrabu: When a Small Estate Grows and the Question of Excess Benefit

micro_segments.title: The question posed: if the estate was insufficient but then grows, do daughters keep the full increased value or do boys get the excess?

macro_segments.title: Merubim Venismatu: When a Large Estate Shrinks and the Status of the Heirs' Portion (continued)

micro_segments.title: The role of actual possession and kinyan: whether formal assignment of property changes the debt-paid-off analysis

macro_segments.title: Muatim Venisrabu: When a Small Estate Grows and the Question of Excess Benefit (continued)

micro_segments.title: Two models: creditor model (daughters collect only their debt) versus property ownership model (daughters own the increased value)
Distinction between daughters as true creditors versus ongoing support obligation: the Tosafot-Rashbam debate on when property transfers
The sons' ability to sell: evidence that daughters have a lien, not ownership, because the sale is valid and sons retain control
Rashbam versus Tosafot: when does court seizure (geviya) prevent further sales and give daughters true ownership?

macro_segments.title: The Widow and Multiple Creditors: Priority and the Framework for Assessing Estate Size

micro_segments.title: Introduction: if the estate must support both daughters and a widow, does the widow's obligation reduce the initial estate assessment?
The Gemara's reasoning: widow's support is uncertain (she may remarry) so we don't factor it into initial classification; thus merubim venismatu applies
Stepdaughter from prior marriage: does her defined support obligation make her automatically reduce estate size?
Common creditor (ba'al chov): does a debt owed to an external creditor reduce initial estate assessment or apply only post-hoc?
Reversal framing: the Gemara asks the question in opposite directions to probe whether the conclusion favors protecting heirs or reducing them
Widow versus daughters priority: widow receives support first; daughters only if funds remain after widow is supported

macro_segments.title: Admon's Argument: Do Sons Deserve Equal Support as Daughters in a Small Estate?

micro_segments.title: Admon's claim: because I am male and suited for Torah study, I should not lose out on support when the estate is small
Rava's rejection: spiritual capability (Torah learning) does not determine legal inheritance rights; that conflates religion with property law
Admon's true legal argument: males are privileged in large estates (inherit the bulk), so why lose out in small ones?
The counter-logic: males have greater ability to earn; females need protected support; different rights in different scenarios
Tosafot's view: Admon is merely pointing out the irony, not making a binding halachic claim; Rashbam rules like Admon

macro_segments.title: Tumtum and Androgynous: Indeterminate Sex in Inheritance and Conditional Gifts

micro_segments.title: Definition: tumtum (indeterminate sex, genitalia not descended or visible) versus androgynous (both sets present); both create legal complexity
Tumtum in a large estate: sons try to classify him as daughter (to limit share); daughters try to classify as son (to limit his support)
Tumtum in a small estate: the female heirs can reject his claim for support, forcing him to beg despite uncertain status
Conditional gifts to an unborn child: if boy gets 100, if girl gets 200, and both conditions are met
Standard reading of twins scenario: both get their respective amounts; Rashbam disagrees, saying unforeseen twins void the condition
Tumtum in conditional gifts: neither condition met; tumtum as its own category (beriya bifnei atzmah), not a safek between male and female

topical_tags.term: lien model / creditor status of daughters
kvar zachu bahem yorshin (heirs already acquired their portion)
Tosafot vs. Rashbam (on geviya, on Admon ruling)
beriya bifnei atzmah (tumtum as distinct category, not safek)
conditional gifts to unborn child (matnat shechiv mera analogy)
Rashbam's reading of 'zachar unekeiva' (or vs. and in twins)
